content.text: There is a file called nul in the work tree here that was added by something that I'm unable to remove because it cannot be found. However, when I look at it and explore, etc., I can see it has zero bytes and was made in 1970. Can you find it and remove that for me?

That `nul` file is a classic Windows gotcha: `NUL` is a reserved DOS device name, so normal file APIs resolve it to the null device (hence 0 bytes, epoch timestamp) instead of the actual directory entry, and regular delete fails. It was probably created by a command redirecting to `nul` from a non-cmd shell. Deleting it requires the `\\?\` path prefix, which bypasses the reserved-name translation — let me do that.

input.description: Delete reserved-name 'nul' file using \\?\ path prefix

content.text:
Gone. The delete via `\\?\D:\oh-my-posh\nul` succeeded, and `git status` now shows only your `apm.lock.yaml` and `apm.yml` modifications — the untracked `nul` entry is no longer there.

For future reference: this file typically appears when something redirects output to `nul` (cmd-style) from PowerShell or another context where `nul` isn't treated as the device, creating a real file with the reserved name. The `\\?\` prefix tells Windows to skip DOS device-name translation, which is why it can see and delete the actual directory entry when Explorer and plain `del`/`Remove-Item` can't.
